covid_lungs: Distinguished by bilateral, peripheral ground-glass opacities and patchy consolidations, unlike other lung diseases.
COVID-19 lungs often show multifocal infiltrates and diffuse involvement, setting them apart from normal and non-COVID pneumonia.
The radiographic pattern is more likely to be peripheral and lower lobe predominant compared to other viral pneumonias.
Unlike lung opacity of other causes, COVID-19 is associated with specific epidemiological and clinical features.
COVID-19 lungs are differentiated from normal lungs by the presence of widespread opacities and from viral pneumonia by their distribution and severity.
lung_opacity_lungs: Characterized by areas of increased radiodensity, lung opacity is non-specific and can result from various conditions.
Unlike COVID-19 or viral pneumonia, lung opacity may be focal, diffuse, unilateral, or bilateral without a specific pattern.
This class is set apart by the presence of abnormal whiteness or cloudiness, not necessarily linked to infection.
Lung opacity is distinguished from normal lungs by the loss of clear lung fields.
Unlike COVID-19, the cause of opacity in this class is not defined and requires further clinical correlation.
normal_lungs: Shows clear lung fields with no evidence of disease, unlike all pathological classes.
Normal lungs are free of opacities, consolidations, or infiltrates seen in COVID-19 and viral pneumonia.
This class is distinguished by sharp costophrenic angles and visible vascular markings.
Unlike all other classes, normal lungs have no abnormal radiodensity or structural changes.
Normal lungs serve as a baseline for comparison with all diseased lung images.
viral_pneumonia_lungs: Characterized by patchy or diffuse infiltrates and ground-glass opacities, viral pneumonia is distinct from normal and COVID-19 lungs.
Unlike COVID-19, viral pneumonia may have less peripheral and more central involvement.
This class is set apart by radiographic features typical of viral infections, excluding those specific to COVID-19.
Viral pneumonia lungs often show multifocal involvement, but the pattern may differ from COVID-19.
Unlike lung opacity, viral pneumonia is defined by clinical and radiological evidence of viral infection.
